data.description:

Approach 1: Uniti Group Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model looks at the cash Uniti Group is expected to generate in the future, then discounts those projected cash flows back to today to estimate what the business might be worth right now.

For Uniti Group, the latest twelve month free cash flow is a loss of $146.97 million. Analyst inputs and Simply Wall St extrapolations then project annual free cash flow out to 2035, shifting from negative figures in the nearer years to a projected $205.04 million in 2030. Amounts are relatively modest, so it is helpful to keep in mind these are in millions of dollars rather than billions.

Using these projections in a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity model produces an estimated intrinsic value of about $7.49 per share. Against a recent share price of $7.50, the model suggests Uniti Group is around 0.2% overvalued, which is effectively in line with its DCF estimate.

Approach 2: Uniti Group Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful way to see how much you are paying for each dollar of earnings. It links the share price directly to the bottom line, which is usually what matters most over time.

What counts as a “normal” P/E depends on how investors see growth prospects and risk. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can support a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk usually justifies a lower one.

Uniti Group currently trades on a P/E of 1.13x. That sits well below the Telecom industry average of 16.44x and below the peer average of 10.70x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Uniti Group is 3.14x, which is its proprietary estimate of what a reasonable P/E could be, given factors like earnings growth, profit margins, industry, market cap and company specific risks.

The Fair Ratio is more tailored than a simple peer or industry comparison because it adjusts for those company specific drivers rather than assuming all Telecom stocks deserve similar multiples.
